claim: Adult tissue-resident macrophages possess a self-renewing capacity.

result.claim: Adult tissue-resident macrophages possess a self-renewing capacity.
result.reasoning: Multiple studies provide strong and consistent evidence that adult tissue-resident macrophages possess a self-renewing capacity. Paper ID 12827098 explicitly states that "Tissue-resident macrophages self-maintain locally throughout adult life with minimal contribution from circulating monocytes" (Proposition 2). This paper further elaborates that after depletion, repopulation occurs by "stochastic cellular proliferation in situ" (Proposition 4) and that "host macrophages retained the capacity to expand" (Proposition 3). Similarly, Paper ID 6446747 demonstrates that "MafB/c-Maf deficiency enables self-renewal of differentiated functional macrophages" (Proposition 6) and "extended expansion of mature monocytes and macrophages in culture" (Proposition 12). Paper ID 7521113 supports this by indicating that major tissue-resident macrophage populations "maintain themselves subsequently during adulthood independent of replenishment by blood monocytes" (Proposition 1). Finally, Paper ID 22406695 reinforces the idea that "most adult tissue macrophages originate during embryonic development and not from circulating monocytes" (Proposition 5), implying a self-maintenance mechanism. The evidence from these papers consistently supports the claim.

evidence.text: Nac1 is a self-renewal promoting factor, but is not required for maintaining pluripotency of ESCs.
evidence.source: Nac1 promotes self-renewal of embryonic stem cells through direct transcriptional regulation of c-Myc

evidence.text: After bone marrow transplantation, host macrophages retained the capacity to expand when the development of donor macrophages was compromised.
evidence.source: Tissue-resident macrophages self-maintain locally throughout adult life with minimal contribution from circulating monocytes.

evidence.text: The extended expansion of MafB/c-Maf deficient macrophages occurs without loss of differentiated phenotype and function.
evidence.source: MafB/c-Maf deficiency enables self-renewal of differentiated functional macrophages.

evidence.text: MSC obtained from different human tissues have tissue repair and immunomodulatory properties.
evidence.source: Mesenchymal stromal (stem) cells to improve solid organ transplant outcome: lessons from the initial clinical trials.

evidence.text: Major tissue-resident macrophage populations, including liver Kupffer cells and lung alveolar, splenic, and peritoneal macrophages, are established prior to birth.
evidence.source: Fate mapping reveals origins and dynamics of monocytes and tissue macrophages under homeostasis.

evidence.text: Monocytes do not show significant contribution to tissue macrophages in the steady state.
evidence.source: Tissue-resident macrophages self-maintain locally throughout adult life with minimal contribution from circulating monocytes.

evidence.text: Adult stem cells have a finite replication potential.
evidence.source: Human myotubes from myoblast cultures undergoing senescence exhibit defects in glucose and lipid metabolism

evidence.text: Murine ES cells can be maintained as a pluripotent, self-renewing population by LIF/STAT3-dependent signaling.
evidence.source: LIF/STAT3 controls ES cell self-renewal and pluripotency by a Myc-dependent mechanism.

evidence.text: Continuous proliferation of MafB/c-Maf deficient macrophages requires concomitant up-regulation of KLF4 and c-Myc.
evidence.source: MafB/c-Maf deficiency enables self-renewal of differentiated functional macrophages.

evidence.text: Repopulation after lung macrophage depletion occurred in a macrophage colony-stimulating factor (M-Csf)- and granulocyte macrophage (GM)-CSF-dependent manner.
evidence.source: Tissue-resident macrophages self-maintain locally throughout adult life with minimal contribution from circulating monocytes.

evidence.text: MafB and c-Maf deficiency enables extended expansion of mature monocytes and macrophages in culture.
evidence.source: MafB/c-Maf deficiency enables self-renewal of differentiated functional macrophages.

evidence.text: Classical Ly6c(hi) monocytes patrol the extravascular space in resting organs.
evidence.source: Origin and functions of tissue macrophages.

evidence.text: Inflammation triggers monocytes to differentiate into macrophages.
evidence.source: Origin and functions of tissue macrophages.

evidence.text: Adiponectin acts on blood vessels to inhibit macrophage inflammation.
evidence.source: Fibroblast Growth Factor 21 Prevents Atherosclerosis by Suppression of Hepatic Sterol Regulatory Element-Binding Protein-2 and Induction of Adiponectin in Mice

evidence.text: Wnt proteins in combination with the cytokine LIF are sufficient to support ESC self-renewal.
evidence.source: Embryonic stem cells require Wnt proteins to prevent differentiation to epiblast stem cells

evidence.text: TFEB was rapidly activated in murine macrophages upon Staphylococcus aureus infection.
evidence.source: Innate host defense requires TFEB-mediated transcription of cytoprotective and antimicrobial genes.

evidence.text: Mononuclear phagocytes include monocytes, macrophages, and dendritic cells.
evidence.source: Fate mapping reveals origins and dynamics of monocytes and tissue macrophages under homeostasis.

evidence.text: Most adult tissue macrophages originate during embryonic development.
evidence.source: Origin and functions of tissue macrophages.

evidence.text: After depletion of lung macrophages, the majority of repopulation occurred by stochastic cellular proliferation in situ.
evidence.source: Tissue-resident macrophages self-maintain locally throughout adult life with minimal contribution from circulating monocytes.

evidence.text: Expansion of host macrophages prevented the development of alveolar proteinosis in mice transplanted with GM-Csf-receptor-deficient progenitors.
evidence.source: Tissue-resident macrophages self-maintain locally throughout adult life with minimal contribution from circulating monocytes.
